agent1_snippets.title: Disease-Modifying Antirheumatic Drugs (DMARD) -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: DMARDs are immunosuppressive and immunomodulatory agents and are classified as either conventional DMARDs or biologic DMARDs. Commonly used conventional DMARDs include methotrexate, leflunomide, hydroxychloroquine, and sulfasalazine. Biologic DMARDs were introduced in the early 1990s and are usually prescribed after the failure of conventional DMARD therapy (ongoing disease activity or clinical or radiographic disease progression). Some biologic agents include infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept, rituximab, abatacept, rituximab, tocilizumab, tofacitinib, among others. Biologic DMARDs are highly specific and target a specific pathway of the immune system. Some of these drugs are monoclonal, chimeric humanized fusions antibodies, while others are receptors that have been fused to a part of the human immunoglobulin or small molecules such as Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors.

agent1_snippets.title: Disease-Modifying Antirheumatic Drugs (DMARD) -- Monitoring
agent1_snippets.content: Complete blood count and liver function test shall be performed monthly for at least 3 months initially, and every 2 to 3 months thereafter in patients initiated on agents including methotrexate, leflunomide, sulfasalazine, tocilizumab, tofacitinib, and sarilumab. Lipid panel shall be monitored at baseline and then every 3 months for at least 6 months and then every 6 months thereafter in patients initiated on tocilizumab, tofacitinib, and sarilumab. CBC shall be monitored every 6 months in patients on any of the biologic DMARDs. Since many of these agents require dose adjustment in renal insufficiency, close monitoring of renal function every 3 to 6 months is also recommended in patients on DMARDs. Comprehensive ophthalmology exams including visual field testing and ocular coherence tomography are necessary at baseline, at 5 years, and then annually in patients on hydroxychloroquine.

agent1_snippets.title: Tocilizumab -- Contraindications -- Box Warning
agent1_snippets.content: Tocilizumab carries an FDA box warning for an increased risk of serious infections. Many patients taking tocilizumab may also take an immunosuppressant, eg, methotrexate, glucocorticoid, etc. Examples of potential infections are tuberculosis, aspergillosis, candidiasis, cryptococcosis, and varicella-zoster infection. Therefore, the healthcare team needs to monitor for signs of infection.

agent1_snippets.title: Still Disease -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Glucocorticoids serve as the second-line treatment after anakinra, typically at a dose of prednisone 20 to 60 mg orally. In some instances, intraarticular steroids may be considered, and a pulse dose of methylprednisolone 100 mg daily for up to three days is used in patients with MAS or those with refractory systemic or arthritic disease. [21] Limited evidence suggests that canakinumab, rilonacept, rituximab, and abatacept may effectively treat patients refractory to other therapies. Patients should undergo regular monitoring, including periodic assessments of CBC, BUN, creatinine, electrolytes, ferritin, D-dimer, alanine transaminase (ALT), and aspartate  aminotransferase (AST).

agent1_snippets.title: Upadacitinib -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Using upadacitinib with other JAK inhibitors (JAKinibs) or robust immunosuppressants like azathioprine and cyclosporine is not advised. However, its use in combination with nonbiologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate is supported, while its use with biological DMARDs is not recommended (see Image. Jak Inhibitors Table). [3] When combined with the first-line therapy methotrexate, upadacitinib repressed disease progression on radiographic imaging and maintained clinical efficacy. [1]

agent1_snippets.title: Tocilizumab -- Monitoring
agent1_snippets.content: For RA, giant cell arteritis, or SSc-ILD: Patients should have the following baseline tests: hepatitis B serology, including HBsAg, TB test, AST/ALT, alkaline phosphatase, and total bilirubin every 4 to 8 weeks for 6 months, then every 3 months. They should also have a baseline ANC and platelets at 4 to 8 weeks, then every 3 months. Finally, a lipid panel should be performed 4 to 8 weeks following treatment initiation.

agent1_snippets.title: Baricitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Baricitinib is a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) indicated for rheumatoid arthritis when conventional DMARDs prove inadequate. This medication is approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to treat adult patients with moderately to severely active rheumatoid arthritis who have not responded adequately to other DMARDs, including tumor necrosis factor antagonist therapies. Furthermore, baricitinib is FDA-approved for treating COVID-19, presenting a valuable choice in combination with other therapies for hospitalized patients, including pediatric cases necessitating supplemental oxygen, invasive mechanical ventilation, or extracorporeal membrane oxygenation. This activity comprehensively reviews the indications, contraindications, mechanisms, adverse events, and other crucial aspects of baricitinib therapy within the clinical setting. This activity is tailored to meet the specific needs of an interprofessional healthcare team responsible for treating patients with rheumatoid arthritis, alopecia areata, and COVID-19.

agent1_snippets.title: Tocilizumab -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: This activity examines the wide-ranging applications of tocilizumab, a humanized monoclonal antibody approved by the FDA for managing rheumatoid arthritis (RA), giant cell arteritis (GCA), polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis (PJIA), systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (SJIA), and COVID-19 pneumonia. The program elucidates the intricate mechanisms of action underlying tocilizumab's efficacy, detailing its authorized uses across diverse patient populations. Emphasis is placed on the interprofessional healthcare team's pivotal role in comprehending therapeutic considerations, potential adverse effects, and nuanced decision-making associated with tocilizumab, particularly in off-label contexts such as COVID-19 treatment.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
agent2_snippets.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several corticosteroid drugs that share the common function of reducing inflammation in various clinical settings. Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2883
agent2_snippets.content: Gout and other crystal arthropathies are inflammatory joint disorders characterized by the deposition of monosodium urate or other crystals within synovial tissues. In clinical practice, these conditions must be differentiated from rheumatoid arthritis (التهاب المفاصل الروماتويدي) and from التهاب المفاصل المعدي, as both of these diseases can present with joint pain and swelling that mimic crystal‑induced arthritis. Accurate differential diagnosis (ddx) is essential to ensure appropriate management and to avoid misclassification. Moreover, the presence of gout and other crystal arthropathies may serve as a contraindication to the use of triamcinolonacetonid, a corticosteroid preparation, indicating that clinicians should consider alternative therapeutic strategies rather than prescribing this medication to patients with crystal‑related arthritis.

agent2_snippets.content: Corticosteroids, potent (group III) represent a class of high‑potency corticosteroid agents, and amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid are all members of this group. These four compounds share the pharmacologic characteristics of potent group III corticosteroids, which are commonly employed for their strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects in the treatment of dermatologic and other inflammatory conditions. Recognizing that amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid belong to the same potent corticosteroid class helps clinicians select appropriate therapy and anticipate similar efficacy and safety profiles across these agents.

agent2_snippets.content: Systemic glucocorticoids represent a class of corticosteroid medications that includes agents such as prednison and paramethason. Both prednison and paramethason are synthetic glucocorticoid drugs administered systemically to exert potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By belonging to the systemic glucocorticoid category, these agents share common mechanisms of action, pharmacologic properties, and therapeutic indications, making them useful in the management of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions. Understanding that prednison and paramethason are members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate class‑related side effects, and provide comprehensive patient care.
